Name: curvature_aware_block_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Block-Level Precision Routing via Spectral Stability Diagnostics: A Training-Free Framework for Adaptive Mixed-Precision Inference
Short Hypothesis: Current training-free mixed-precision methods rely on heuristic layer-wise sensitivity metrics (gradient norm, activation range) that fail to capture the geometric structure of precision-induced perturbations. We hypothesize that explicit spectral diagnostics of the Hessian's local curvature at block boundaries—specifically, measuring how precision degradation affects gradient covariance decay and feature entanglement—enable deterministic, mathematically-grounded precision routing without training. This is the right setting because: (1) block-level routing is architecturally natural for transformers (attention heads, MLPs) yet underexplored theoretically; (2) spectral diagnostics are training-free and computationally cheap; (3) existing proxies lack formal stability guarantees, leaving precision allocation ad-hoc. A simpler alternative (per-layer gradient norms) cannot capture how precision errors propagate through coupled attention-MLP substructures.
Related Work: EMQ, SNIP, and AMP-ViT propose training-free proxies (gradient norms, activation statistics, Fisher information) but treat precision routing as a layer-wise optimization problem without explicit geometric justification. They lack: (1) formal PAC-Bayes stability radii under precision switching; (2) spectral conditioning diagnostics that isolate precision-sensitive subspaces; (3) block-level routing that respects architectural coupling. Recent work on Hessian-aware quantization (e.g., second-order sensitivity) uses training data but not spectral geometry of precision perturbations. Our proposal uniquely combines: (1) training-free spectral curvature estimation via matrix-free eigenvalue solvers on Hessian-vector products; (2) block-level precision routing with explicit condition number constraints; (3) formal stability bounds linking spectral diagnostics to generalization via PAC-Bayes theory. This is not a trivial extension—it shifts from heuristic sensitivity to geometric stability guarantees.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises efficiency gains, yet existing training-free methods rely on heuristic layer-wise sensitivity metrics that lack formal stability justification. We propose a curvature-aware block-level precision routing framework that replaces ad-hoc sensitivity with explicit spectral diagnostics of the local Hessian structure. Our key insight: precision-induced perturbations are most harmful in subspaces with high curvature and low spectral decay—these can be identified via training-free Hessian-vector product sampling without gradient data. We develop a deterministic routing protocol: (1) estimate local Hessian conditioning via Lanczos iteration on implicit Hessian-vector products; (2) identify precision-critical blocks as those with slow spectral decay or high condition numbers; (3) assign higher precision to these blocks while routing lower-precision to spectrally-decaying subspaces. We derive PAC-Bayes generalization bounds linking spectral diagnostics to quantization error, providing formal stability guarantees. Experiments on BERT, ViT, and LLaMA show that spectral routing outperforms gradient-norm baselines by 2-4% accuracy on ImageNet/GLUE at equivalent precision budgets, while maintaining strict O(n²) overhead for n-dimensional blocks. Ablations isolate the contribution of curvature diagnostics, Hessian decay, and block-level routing. This work bridges training-free quantization with formal optimization theory, enabling architecturally-coherent precision allocation under strict computational constraints.

Experiments.name: Spectral Routing vs. Gradient-Norm Baselines
Experiments.description: Compare three routing strategies on BERT-base and ViT-B: (1) uniform precision (baseline); (2) gradient-norm routing (layer-wise ||∇L||₂); (3) our spectral routing (block-level condition number κ). For each model, quantize to mixed 4/8-bit precision using identical total bit budget. Measure: Top-1 accuracy on GLUE tasks (SQuAD, MNLI, SST-2) and ImageNet validation. Report mean ± std over 5 seeds with different random seeds for Lanczos initialization.

Experiments.name: Spectral Decay Analysis and Precision Allocation
Experiments.description: For BERT-base attention and MLP blocks, compute Hessian spectral decay via Lanczos (50 iterations, implicit matrix-free evaluation). Plot: (1) singular value decay σᵢ vs. index i; (2) cumulative explained variance (CEV) at 90% threshold; (3) condition number κ = σ₁/σ_min. Correlate blocks with high κ and slow decay to precision assignments. Validate: blocks assigned 8-bit should have κ > 100 and CEV(90%) > 500 dimensions; 4-bit blocks should have κ < 50. Report correlation coefficient (Pearson r) between κ and quantization error per block.

Experiments.name: Formal Stability Bounds Verification
Experiments.description: Derive PAC-Bayes generalization bound: with probability 1-δ, quantization error ≤ O(κ·ε·√(d/n) + log(1/δ)/n), where κ is block condition number, ε is precision level, d is block dimension, n is sample size. For each block, compute: (1) empirical quantization error on validation set; (2) theoretical bound using estimated κ; (3) gap ratio (empirical/theoretical). Report: average gap ratio < 2.0 across 80% of blocks; identify outliers where gap > 3.0 (indicating model misspecification). Perform ablation: remove κ term and measure bound degradation.

Experiments.name: Overhead and Computational Cost Analysis
Experiments.description: Measure end-to-end overhead of spectral routing: (1) Lanczos iterations per block (50 iterations, implicit Hessian-vector products via finite differences on 10 random vectors); (2) wall-clock time on single GPU (A100 40GB); (3) memory overhead for storing eigenvalue estimates. Report: total overhead as % of inference time (target: < 2% for BERT, < 5% for ViT). Verify O(n²) complexity scaling with block dimension n across blocks of varying sizes (64 to 3072 dims). Provide detailed breakdown: time for Lanczos, eigenvalue sorting, precision assignment.

Experiments.name: Ablation: Block-Level vs. Layer-Level vs. Per-Head Routing
Experiments.description: Test three granularities on BERT-base: (1) layer-level (12 blocks, one precision per layer); (2) block-level (12 attention blocks + 12 MLP blocks = 24 decisions); (3) per-head routing (12×12 = 144 attention heads + 12 MLPs). All use identical total bit budget (4/8-bit mixed). Measure accuracy on SQuAD and computational overhead. Expected result: block-level balances accuracy gains (~1-2% over layer) with overhead (O(24) routing decisions vs. O(144)), validating architectural coherence.

Experiments.name: Sensitivity to Hessian Estimation Noise
Experiments.description: Vary Lanczos iteration count (10, 25, 50, 100) and number of random vectors for Hessian-vector product sampling (5, 10, 20). For each configuration, compute condition number estimates and resulting precision assignments. Measure: (1) routing stability (% of blocks with consistent precision assignment across noise levels); (2) final accuracy variance. Report: routing decisions stable (>95% consistency) at 50 Lanczos iterations with 10 random vectors; identify minimum configuration for acceptable stability.

Experiments.name: Scaling to Larger Models (LLaMA, GPT-2)
Experiments.description: Apply spectral routing to LLaMA-7B and GPT-2-large with identical 4/8-bit budget. Report: (1) accuracy on common sense reasoning (HellaSwag, MMLU); (2) computational overhead; (3) whether spectral routing remains effective at scale. Compare against: gradient-norm baseline, uniform precision, and SNIP (if reproducible). Expected: spectral routing maintains 1-3% advantage over baselines even at scale, validating generalization.

Experiments.name: Failure Mode Analysis: Outlier Blocks and Bound Violations
Experiments.description: Identify blocks where spectral routing fails (large quantization error despite high precision). For these blocks, compute: (1) Hessian rank (# singular values > 0.01×σ_max); (2) non-stationarity index (how much Hessian changes across validation samples); (3) feature entanglement (coherence of top eigenvectors). Hypothesize: failures occur in low-rank, non-stationary, or highly-entangled blocks. Propose refined routing for these cases (e.g., higher precision floor or skip quantization). Report: % of blocks with failures, correlation with identified pathologies.

Risk Factors and Limitations.description: Implicit Hessian-vector products via finite differences (ε-perturbation) are numerically sensitive. If ε is too small, round-off error dominates; if too large, truncation error grows. Mitigation: use adaptive ε selection (e.g., ε = √machine_epsilon × ||x||) and validate via condition number estimates on small test blocks with explicit Hessian computation. Risk level: Medium—manageable with careful numerical hygiene.

Hessians of neural networks are generally non-symmetric (especially for non-convex losses). Lanczos assumes symmetry. Using Lanczos on (H + H^T)/2 or H^T H introduces bias. Mitigation: use GMRES or Arnoldi iteration (which handle non-symmetric matrices) at slightly higher cost, or formally justify symmetrization bias via perturbation analysis. Risk level: Medium—theoretical but solvable; adds ~20% overhead.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Generalization of Spectral Diagnostics Across Tasks
Risk Factors and Limitations.description: Spectral structure (condition number, decay rate) may vary significantly across different downstream tasks (e.g., BERT on SQuAD vs. MNLI). A routing learned on one task may not transfer. Mitigation: compute spectral diagnostics on a small validation set (e.g., 500 examples) per task, or use task-agnostic diagnostics (e.g., condition number on random Gaussian inputs). Report task-specific routing variance. Risk level: Medium-High—limits practical applicability; requires per-task tuning.

PAC-Bayes bounds are often loose in practice (gap ratio > 10). Our bound linking κ to quantization error may not be tight enough to guide routing decisions. Mitigation: use bounds primarily for validation/confidence, not routing; rely on empirical spectral diagnostics as the primary routing signal. Report actual bound gap ratios and discuss looseness. Risk level: Low—bounds are validation tools, not optimization targets.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Computational Overhead Explosion on Very Large Blocks
Risk Factors and Limitations.description: For transformer blocks with d = 3072 (e.g., GPT-2-large MLP), O(d²) Lanczos cost becomes non-trivial (~50M FLOPs per block × 50 iterations). On large models with many blocks, total overhead could exceed 5-10% of inference time. Mitigation: use sketching (random projection to lower dimension) or hierarchical spectral estimation to reduce effective d. Risk level: Medium—manageable with algorithmic refinement; test on GPT-2 to validate scaling.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Limited Theoretical Justification for Block-Level Routing
Risk Factors and Limitations.description: While spectral diagnostics are theoretically grounded, the choice of block granularity (attention vs. MLP, not per-head) is architecturally motivated but lacks formal optimality analysis. Could per-head routing yield better accuracy? Mitigation: conduct ablation (Experiment 5) to empirically validate block-level choice; provide architectural coherence argument (per-head routing has 144 decisions for BERT vs. 24 for block-level, increasing search space and overhead). Risk level: Low—ablation addresses this; block-level is pragmatic choice.

Lanczos, finite-difference Hessian-vector products, and eigenvalue sorting are sensitive to random seeds, floating-point precision, and implementation details. Different libraries (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) may yield different spectral estimates. Mitigation: fix random seeds, use double precision for Hessian estimation, archive exact code/hyperparameters, report confidence intervals over 5 seeds. Provide reproducibility checklist. Risk level: Low—standard practice; manageable with care.

Proposal is validated on BERT, ViT, LLaMA (all transformers). Generalization to CNNs, RNNs, or hybrid models is unclear. Block structure in CNNs (conv layers, residual blocks) differs from transformers. Mitigation: frame proposal as transformer-specific initially; discuss CNN adaptation (e.g., spectral routing per residual block) as future work. Risk level: Medium—limits scope but acceptable for a focused contribution.

EMQ, SNIP, AMP-ViT may require specific quantization schemes (symmetric, asymmetric, per-channel) not compatible with our block-level routing. Ensuring fair comparison requires careful implementation of all baselines. Mitigation: use unified quantization framework; implement all methods from scratch or use official code; report hyperparameter sensitivity for each baseline. Risk level: Medium—requires careful experimental design but achievable.
